title: 中國與其島嶼之鳥類目錄修正版，包括新種描述、過往注釋參照與評註
alternative: A revised catalogue of the birds of China and its islands, with descriptions of new species, references to former notes, and occasional remarks）
description: 史溫侯補充中國沿海與附近島嶼所見鳥種，以下物種為可在福爾摩沙所見，朱鸝臺灣亞種（Psaropholus ardens, Swinh.，目前學名Oriolus traillii）白肩鵰（Aquila bifasciata, J. E. Gray.，目前學名Aquila heliaca）、東方鵟（Buteo asiaticus, Lath.）、熊鷹（Spizaetus nipalensis, Hodgs.，目前學名Nisaetus nipalensis）、大冠鷲（Spilornis cheela, Daud.）、魚鷹（Pandion haliaetus, L.）、遊隼（Falco peregrinus, L.）、紅隼（Tinnunculus alaudarius, var. japonicus.，目前學名Falco tinnunculus）、黑鳶南亞亞種（Milvus govinda, Sykes，目前學名Milvus migrans）、鳳頭蒼鷹（Lophospiza trivirgata, Cuv.，目前學名Accipiter trivirgatus）、鵂鶹臺灣亞種（Athene pardalota，目前學名Glaucidium brodiei pardalotum）、褐鷹鴞（Ninox japonica, Temm. et Schleg.）、黃嘴角鴞臺灣亞種（Lempijius hambroecki，目前學名Otus spilocephalus hambroecki）、褐林鴞臺灣亞種（Bulaca nemvarensis, Hodgs.，目前學名Strix leptogrammica caligata）、東方草鴞（Strix candida, Tickell，目前學名Tyto longimembris）、南亞夜鷹臺灣亞種（Caprimulgus stictomus，目前學名Caprimulgus affinis stictomus）、小雨燕（Cypselus subfurcatus, Blyth，目前學名Apus nipalensis）、家燕白腹亞種（Hirundo gutturalis, Scop.，目前學名Hirundo rustica gutturalis）、赤腰燕（Cecropis striolata, Temm. & Schleg.）、赤翡翠（Halcyon coromandeliana, Scop.，目前學名Halcyon coromanda）、棕扇尾鶯（Cisticola schoenicola, Bp.，目前學名Cisticola juncidis）、黃頭扇尾鶯臺灣亞種（Cisticola volitans, Swinh.，目前學名Cisticola exilis volitans）、遠東樹鶯（Herbivox minuta，目前學名Horornis canturians）、短尾鶯（Tribura squamiceps, Swinhoe，目前學名Urosphena squameiceps、黑喉鴝（Pratincola indica, Blyth，目前學名Saxicola maurus）、青背山雀臺灣亞種（Parus insperatus, Swinh.，目前學名Parus monticolus insperatus）、赤腹山雀（Parus castaneoventris, Gould，目前學名Sittiparus castaneoventris）、東方黃鶺鴒黃眉亞種（Budytes taivanus, Swinh.，目前學名Motacilla tschutschensis taivana）、赤腹鶇（Turdus chrysolaus, Temm.）、白眉鶇（Turdus obscurus, Gmel.）、藍磯鶇棕腹亞種（Petrocincla manilla, Bodd.，目前學名Monticola solitarius philippensis）、 白耳畫眉（Sibia auricularis, Swinh.，目前學名Heterophasia auricularis）、小彎嘴（Pomatorhinus musicus, Swinh.）、臺灣白喉噪鶥（Garrulax ruficeps, Gould，目前學名 Ianthocincla ruficeps）、粉紅鸚嘴臺灣亞種（Suthora bulomachus，Swinh.，目前學名Sinosuthora webbiana bulomacha）、朱鸝臺灣亞種（Psaropholus ardens, Swinh.，目前學名Oriolus traillii），全部名單總計675種。

Proceedings of the Scientific Meetings of the Zoological Society of London (1871): 337-423.（羅伯特・史溫侯。〈中國與其島嶼之鳥類目錄修正版，包括新種描述、過往注釋參照與評註〉，《倫敦動物學會集刊》（1871年）第337-423頁。）

languageDocuments.text: ##--SPLIT--##[From the PROCEEDINGS OF THE ZOOLOGICAL Society of London. May 2, 1871.] A Revised Catalogue of the Birds of China and its Islands, with Descriptions of New Species, References to former Notes, and occasional Remarks. By ROBERT SWINHOE, F.Z.S.     1. GTYPAETUS BARBATUS, Cuv. ; Ibis, 1867, p. 413 ; P. Z. S.  1870, p. 430.     I have seen, in the museum attached to the Catholic Mission at Pekin, a Bearded Vulture, procured by Pere Armand David in the mountains west of that city. There is also a specimen of it in the British Museum, marked " China." ##--SPLIT--##    2. VULTUR MONACHUS, L. ; Ibis, 1867, p. 413.     There are specimens of this Vulture in the Peking Museum, procured by Père David from the mountains in the north-west of the Chelee Province.     3. AQUILA CHRYSAËTOS, Cuv. ; P. Z. S.  1870, p. 443.     Eagles were several times seen by myself in the Nankow Pass, on the road from Peking to Mongolia, but I was not able to determine the species. Père David identifies the resident species of these mountains with the Golden Eagle, and mentions meeting with a large variety, with longer bill and white tarse (" Catalogue des oiseaux à Pèkin," Nouv. Arch. du Musèum d'Hist. Nat. de Paris, tome iii. 1867), which Mr. Gurney tells me he has seen from Denmark and also from Algeria. Mr. Gurney adds that " the specimens were in other respects in the usual plumage of the second year. The Danish examples were certainly large and fine, the Algerian not particularly so. At present I have seen no sufficient difference to make me think that there are two species or races of Golden Eagle."     4. AQUILA BIFASCIATA, J. E. Gray.     Aquila heliaca, P. Z. S.  1863, p. 259 ; Ibis, 1865, p. 347.    Mr. Howard Saunders has demonstrated to this Society * that the eastern form of Imperial Eagle differs, in its first plumage at least, from the European bird, and is apparently a distinct species. M. Milne-Edwards refers to Dr. Gray's name a bird sent from Pekin by Père David, on which the Abbé remarks, " common on our mountains and in Mongolia." This species has been shot in the immature plumage in winter at Foochow ; and I have noticed it in adult plumage with white scapulars on Ape's Hill, Takow, S.W. Formosa.     5. AQUILA AMURENSIS, sp. nov.     Similis A. clangae, Pall., sed major.     M. Milne-Edwards identifies the Tawny Eagle from Peking with A. clanga of Pallas ; and Père David notes the occurrence also at the same locality of the smaller allied form, A. ncevia, Briss. A specimen from the Amoor, kindly sent me by Dr. v. Schrenck, is of large size, and, in Mr. Gurney's opinion, worthy of separation as a third species of this group. I enter this in the present list under the supposition that it will be the same as No. 5 of Père David's catalogue, which he also states to be " common in our mountains and in Mongolia." Mr. Gurney very obligingly compared the Amoor specimen with a female of Aquila clanga from Sarepta, on the Volga, and has given me the following measurements : Entire length.Wing from carpel joint.Tail.Tarsus.Middle toe without claw.A. clanga ♀ (ad.)31 in.23 in.11 in.31/2 in.21/4 in.A. amurensis ♀ (ad.)331/2 in.261/4 in.121/2 in.4 1/2 in.21/4 in.Mr. Gurney writes:--" In plumage, I do not find that it differs* See anteà,, p. 37.##--SPLIT--##from the adult of A. clanga, except in having the pale transverse, bars on the secondaries lighter in their hue, and therefore raore cou. spicuous, especially on the outer webs.     " It would seem that there are three races of Eagles, only differing in comparative size :--    "Aquila ncevia. The smallest of the three, found in Egypt, Southern Europe, Smyrna, Palestine, India, and Sumatra (one specimen) .     " Aquila clanga, Pall. The intermediate race, found at Sarepta, near the mouth of the Volga. Both it and the preceding have been obtained in European Turkey and in Palestine. I have also seen the Sarepta race from Mesopotamia ; but how far it extends eastward I do not know.     "Aquila (from Amoorland). The largest race. How far it extends westward I do not know."     6. HALIAETCS ALBICILLA (L.) ; Ibis, 1865, p. 347; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 259.     From the river Yangtsze northwards. Seen only occasionally in the south. An immature bird flew on board a ship in the harbour of Swatow, and allowed itself to be captured, in the winter of 1867-68. It fell into my hands. Its iris was clear yellowish brown ; cere light bluish grey ; feet lemon-yellow, with black claws.     7. HALIAËTUS PELAGICUS, Pall. ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260.     Père David saw this giant Eagle once at Peking, and says that it is well known to the Chinese.     8. ARCHIBUTEO AQUILINUS, Hodgs.     Butaquila aquilina et B. leucocephala, Hodgs.     Butaquila hemilasius, Schleg. Faun. Jap.     Buteo lagopus, David, Catalogue des oiseaux à Pékin.    Falco hemilasius, Temm. et Schleg. Faun. Jap.     In January 1868 a large male Buzzard was shot by Consul Caine at Swatow. Mr. Gurney has determined it to be of this species. Its cere was greenish yellow ; iris straw-yellow, redder round the pupil ; legs dingy orange-tinted yellow, with black claws. It is not in mature plumage. Père David has sent specimens of the same to the Paris Museum, and writes that it is not rare at Pekin and breeds there.     9. BUTEO ASIATICUS (Lath.).     Buteo japonicus, Temm. et Schleg. Faun. Japon. ; Zoologist, 1858, p. 6227 ; Ibis, 1860, p. 46, 1861, pp. 24, 326, 1863, pp. 210, 1870, p. 87 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260.     South China to Hainan, and Formosa in winter.     10. SPIZAËTUS NIPALENSIS, Hodgs.     Spizaëtus orientalis, Temm. et Schleg. Faun. Japon.; Swinhoe, Ibis, 1863, p. 211.     Formosa. ##--SPLIT--##    11. SPILORNIS CHEELA (Daud.).     Spilornis hoya, Swinhoe, Ibis, 1866, pp. 304, 307, 399, 1867, pp. 409, 410.     Formosa; Araoy (China).     12. SPILORNIS RUTHERFORDI, Swinhoe, Ibis, 1870, p. 85.      Hainan.     13. POLIORNIS POLIOGENYS (Temm. et Schleg.) ; Swinhoe, Ibis, 1863, p. 88, 1864, p. 429, 1865, p. 545, 1866, p. 136, 1867, p. 411; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 315, 1863, p. 260, 1870, p. 600.    Buteo pygmaeus, Blyth, Ibis, 1865, p. 29.     Formosa ; coast of China. Goes as far south in winter as the Tenasserim Provinces.     14. PANDION HALIAKTUS (L.); Swinhoe, Zoologist, 1858, p. 6227, 1860, p. 7102, 1861, p. 7642, 1864, p. 9224 ; Ibis, 1860, p. 46, 1861, p. 24, 1863, p. 209, 1865, p. 347, 1870, p. 86 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260.     Throughout China, Formosa, and Hainan.     15. FALCO PEREGRINUS, L. ; Zool. 1858, p. 6226; Ibis, 1860, p. 46, 1861, p. 24, 1863, p. 210, 1867, p. 388, 1870, p. 84; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260.     China, Formosa, and Hainan.     16. FALCO SACER, Schlegel; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260. 	Buteo -- ?, Ibis, 1861, p. 326, 1863, p. 88.     Pekin.     17. HYPOTRIORCHIS SUBBUTEO (L.) ; Ibis, 1860, p. 46, 1863, p. 89.    Falco subbuteo, P. Z. S.  1862, p. 315, 1863, p. 260.     Amoy ; Pekin.     18. HYPOTRIORCHIS JESALON (L).     Falco cesalon, Ibis, 1861, p. 327 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260.     Amoy ; Pekin.     19. TlNNUNCULUS ALAUDARIUS, Var. JAPONICUS.     Tinnunculus alaudarius, Swinh. Ibis, 1860, p. 47, 1861, p. 24, 1866, p. 293, 1867, p. 385, 1870, p. 84.     Falco tinnunculus, Ibis, 1861, p. 327.     Tinnunculus japonicus, Ibis, 1863, p. 211, 1864, p. 424 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261.     China, Formosa, and Hainan.     20. ERYTHROPUS AMURENSIS (Radde); Gurney, Ibis, 1868, p. 41, pl. 11 ; P. Z. S.  1870, pp. 436, 448.     Erythropus vespertinus, Ibis, 1861, pp. 253, 327 ; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 315, 1863, p. 260.                 ##--SPLIT--##    Summers at Pekin, and collects in large numbers in September before going south. Wanders in winter to India and Africa.     21. TlCHORNIS PEKINENSIS.     Falco cenchris, var. pekinensis, Swinhoe, P. Z. S.  1870, pp. 442, 448.     Breeds on the western hills of Pekin, and assembles in large numbers in September. Mr. Hodgson's drawing of the Nepal bird (in the British Museum) shows that it is this race of Tichornis cenchris that resorts to India.     The adult male has all the wing-coverts grey right up to the scapulars ; the inner or short primaries are broadly bordered at their tips with whitish, rufous in the immature ; the white on the under quills is 3^ inches short of their tips.     22. PERNIS APIVORUS (L.); David, Catalogue des oiseaux à Pékin.     Père David notes this as found about Pekin in autumn. Schlegel has it from Japan (Musee des Pays-Bas, Pernes, p. 2).     23. MILVUS GOVINDA, Sykes ; Swinh. Zoolog. 1858, p. 6227; Ibis, 1860, p. 47, 1861, pp. 25, 253, 326, 1863, p. 210, 1865, p. 546, 1866, p. 121, 1867, p. 236, 1870, p. 88.     Milvus melanotis, Ibis, 1865, p. 348, 1867, pp. 409, 411; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 260.     China, Formosa, and Hainan.     24. MILVUS MIGRANS (Bodd.).     Milvus ater, Gm.     A live specimen of this Kite, said to have been brought from North China, is now living in the Society's gardens.     25. ASTUR PALUMBARIUS (L.) ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261, 1870, p. 448.     I have an adult male from Pekin, which does not differ from home-shot specimens.     26. LOPHOSPIZA TRIVIRGATA (Cuv.) ; Temm. Pl. Col. 303 ; Ibis, 1865, p. 547, 1866, p. 395.     Formosa. The Malay form.     27. ACCIPITER NISUS (L.) ; Zoolog. 1858, p. 6227; Ibis, 1861, p. 327, 1870, p. 84 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261, 1870, p. 443.     Accipiter -- ? Ibis, 1860, pp. 47, 429, 1863, p. 88.     Peking, Amoy, and Canton.     28. ACCIPITER STEVENSONI, Gurney ; Ibis, 1863, p. 447, pl. 1 1 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261, 1870, p. 600.     Micronisus, sp. ?, Ibis, 1861, p. 25.     Accipiter gularis ?, Ibis, 1863, p. 89.     Canton to Pekin. ##--SPLIT--##    29. ACCIPITER GULARIS, Schleg. F. J. t. 2; Gurney,Ibis, 1865, pp. 236, 547; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261.     Accipiter nisoides, Blyth, Ibis, 1865, p. 28.     Formosa.     30. ACCIPITER VIRGATUS, Temm. Pl. Col. 109 ; Ibis, 1865, p. 108, 1866, p. 405.     Micronisus gularis, Ibis, 1863, p. 212.     Formosa.     31. MICRONISUS BADIUS (Gm.) ; Swinh. Ibis, 1870, p. 84.      Hainan.     32. MICRONISUS SOLOENSIS (Horsf.) ; Pl. Col. 110, 129; Ibis, 1863, p. 89, 1866, p. 403; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 315, 1863, p. 261.     Micronisus badius, Ibis, 1860, p. 359.     Accipiter virgatus, Ibis, 1861, p. 264.     Amoy ; Pescadores ; Shanghai (Brit. Museum) ; Pekin.    33. CIRCUS AERUGINOSUS, L. ; Ibis, 1865, pp. 349, 352, 1870, p. 87.     Swatow ; Amoy ; S.W. Formosa ; Hainan.    34. CIRCUS SPILONOTUS, Kaup ; Ibis, 1863, p. 213, Pl. 5, 1865, pp. 230, 347, 1870, p. 87 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261.     Circus ceruginosus, Ibis, 1860, pp. 47, 359.     Amoy ; river Yangtsze ; Formosa.     35. CIRCUS CYANEUS, L. ; Zoolog. 1858, p. 6227; Ibis, 1860, p. 47, 1861, p. 326, 1870, p. 88 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261.     Amoy.     36. CIRCUS MELANOLEUCUS, Gm. ; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 315, 1863, p. 261.     Circus -- ?, Ibis, 1861, p. 326, 1863, p. 88.     Tientsin.     37. CIRCUS CINERACEUS, Montagu.     River Yangtsze.     38. CIRCUS PALLIDUS, Sykes.     Circus swainsonii, A. Smith ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 261.     River Tangtsze.     39. ATHENE PLUMIPES, Swinhoe, P. Z. S.  1870, p. 448.     Similis A. glauci, sed pedibus dense plumatis.     Not common about the Pekin neighbourhood in autumn ; is found also in the Lake-Baikal region, according to Tacsanowsky of Warsaw.                     ##--SPLIT--##    40. ATHENE WHITELYI, Blyth, Ibis, 1867, p. 313.     Athene cuculoides. Ibis, 1861, pp. 25, 265, 1867, p. 406 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 262.     Ab A. cuculoidi alarum et caudce fasciis paucioribus distinguenda.    Amoy ; Canton ; Foochow ; Szechuen. Goes south on the approach of winter.         41. ATHENE BRODIJEI (Burt.).     Noctua brodicei, Burt. P. Z. S.  1835.     Tingchow (Fokien Province). One specimen of this was brought to me at Amoy in January 1867.     42. ATHENE PARDALOTA, Swinhoe ; Ibis, 1863, p. 216.     Differt a sp. prcecedente venire media nigro guttato.    Formosa.         43. NINOX JAPONICA, Temm. et Schleg. ; Zool. 1858, p. 6228 ; Ibis, 1863, pp. 89, 215, 1864, p. 427, 1866, p. 307, 1870, p. 88; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 316, 1863, p. 262.     Athene scutellata (Raffles) ; Ibis, 1860, p. 47.     Amoy ; Hainan ; Formosa ; Tientsin.     44. BUBO MAXIMUS, Sibb. ; Zool. 1858, p. 6227; Ibis, 1860, p. 47, 1861, pp. 254, 327; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 262.     Amoy, North China.     45. KETUPA CEYLONENSIS (Gmel.); Ibis, 1861, p. 27; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 262.     Hongkong.         46. SCOPS SUNIA, Hodgs.     Scops japonicus, Schleg. Faun. Japon. ; Zool. 1858, p. 6228; Ibis, 1863, p. 89 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 262.     Scops bakkamoena (Penn.); Ibis, 1860, p. 47; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 316.     Amoy in winter; Pekin.     47. LEMPIJIUS SEMITORQUES (Schleg.). 	Ephialtes plumipes, Hume, Scrap Book, Part 1, No. 2, p. 307.     Digitis supra plumatis.     Schlegel (Mus. des Pays-Bas, Oti, p. 24) cites specimens from Japan and Nepal. Père David has sent it from Moupin (N.W. corner of Szechuen ).     48. LEMPIJIUS GLABRIPES.     Ephialtes glabripes, Swinh. Ann. & Mag. Nat. Hist. vol. vi. 4th series, p. 152.     Scops semitorques, Swinh. (nec Schlegel), Ibis, 1863, p. 217, 1867, p. 409; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 262.                 ##--SPLIT--##    Scops -- ?, Ibis, 1861, p. 29.     Scops lempiji, Ibis, 1861, p. 265.     Digitis nudis.     Amoy ; Foochow ; Canton ; Formosa. Differs from the foregoing in having the toes always bare instead of feathered along their upper surfaces.        49. LEMPIJIUS UMBRATILIS, Swinh. Ibis, 1870, p. 342.     Ephialtes lettia, Ibis, 1870, p. 88.      Hainan.     50. LEMPIJIUS HAMBROECKI.     Ephialtes hambroecki, Swinh. Ann. & Mag. Nat. Hist. vol. vi. 4th series, p. 153.     Scops japonicus, Ibis, 1865, p. 348, 1866, p. 307.     Formosa. Of the Lempiji form and type, but small and rufous, and at first sight to be mistaken for Scops japonicus.     51. SYRNIUM NIVICOLUM, Hodgs. J. A. S. xiv. p. 185; P. Z. S.  1870, pp. 438, 443.     Pekin in autumn.     52. PTYNX FULVESCENS (Temm. et Schleg.).     Sent to the Paris Museum by Père David from the Moupin Mountains (N.W. Szechuen).     53. BULACA NEMVARENSIS (Hodgs.).     Bubo caliyatus, Swinh. Ibis, 1863, p. 218, 1864, p. 429.     Formosa.     54. OTUS VULGARIS, Flem. ; Ibis, 1863, p. 89 ; P. Z. S.  1862, p. 316, 1863, p. 262.     Tientsin.     55. OTUS BRACHYOTUS (L.) ; Ibis, 1861, pp. 26,327, 1863, p. 89 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 262.     Peking; Canton.     56. STRIX CANDIDA, Tickell.     Strix pithecops, Swinhoe, Ibis, 1866, pp. 396, 397.     S.W. Formosa.     57. CAPRIMULGUS JOTAKA, Temm. et Schleg. F. J. ; Ibis, 1861, p. 327, 1870, p. 89 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 263, 1870, p. 439.     Caprimulgus -- ?, Ibis, 1860, p. 47, 1861, p. 30.     Caprimulgus dytiscivorus, Swinh. Ibis, 1860, p. 130.     Caprimulgus swinhoeiy Blyth ; Ibis, 1861, p. 263.     China. ##--SPLIT--##    58. CAPRIMULGUS MONTICOLA, Frankl. P. Z. S.  1831, p. 116.     Caprimulgus stictomus, Swinh. P. Z. S.  1863, p. 263.     Caprimulffus pallidus, Gray, Zool. Mise. p. 2.     Caprimulgus -- ?, Ibis, 1860, p 47, 1861, p. 30.     South China in summer. I have a very rufous and brightly marked female from Amoy, which I at first confounded with the following bird.     59. CAPRIMULGUS STICTOMUS, Swinhoe, Ibis, 1863, p. 250, 1866, pp. 315, 402.     Like the last, but smaller, with the toes, especially the middle one, conspicuously longer. Resident in Formosa.     60. CYPSELUS PEKINENSIS, Swinhoe, P. Z. S.  1870, p. 435.     This pale representative of C. apus, L., comes to Pekin in large numbers in April (David} to breed, and leaves early in August. A bird from the Himalayas, brought home by Dr. Jerdon, agrees with my specimens ; and it is probably this species, and not the true C. apus, which is found in India in winter.     61. CYPSELUS PACIFICUS, Lath. Ind. Orn. Suppl. p. 58; Ibis, 1870, p. 89.     Cypselus vittatus, Jard. Ill. Orn. ser. 2. pl. 39 ; Ibis, 1860, pp. 48, 429, 1861, pp. 254, 328, 1863, p. 253, 1865, p. 356; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 263.     Hainan to Pekin, and in Formosa.         62. CYPSELUS SUBFURCATUS, Blyth ; Ibis, 1863, p. 254, 1865, p. 355, 1866, p. 131, 1867, p. 227, 1870, p. 89 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 264.     Cypselus affinis, Ibis, 1860, p. 48, 1861, p. 30.     Hainan to Amoy, and in Formosa.     63. CYPSELUS INFUMATUS, Sclater, P. Z. S.  1865, p. 602.     Cypselus tinus, Swinh. Ibis, 1870, p. 90.     Cypselus tectorum, Jerdon.     Hainan.  I have compared my specimens with Dr. Jerdon's type from Upper Assam, and with Dr. Sclater's type from Borneo in Mr. Wallace's collection, and find them all to be of the same species.     64. S.  1863, p. 264.     From Canton to Pekin. I described in the * Zoologist' for 1858, and in the P. Z. S.  for 1863, the peculiar way in which the Hoopoe produces its notes, by puffing out the sides of its neck and hammering on the ground at the production of each note, thereby exhausting the air at the end of the series of three which make up its song. Before it repeats its call it repeats the puffing of the neck, with a slight gurgling noise. When it is able to strike its bill the sound is the correct " hoo-hoo-hoo ; " but when perched on a rope and only jerking out the song with nods of the head, the notes more resemble the syllables " hoh-hoh-hoh." Mr. Darwin makes use of this last fact to show that some birds have instrumental means to produce their music (Descent of Man, ii. p. 62). It is not to this point, however, that I wish at present to call attention ; but to the fact of the bird's puffing out the sides of its neck. It is generally supposed that the song of a bird is produced by actions of the lower larynx on air passing up the bronchial tubes, onwards and outwards through the main tube or trachea. The trachea of the Hoopoe is not dilatable ; but its oesophagus is ; and the puffing of its neck is caused by the bulging of its oesophagus with swallowed air. There is no connexion between the oesophagus and the trachea, and apparently no organ at the entrance to the former that could modify sound ; what action, then, can this swallowed air be made to take in the production of the bird's notes? Pigeons have strikingly large air-crops, which they empty with each long coo, and refill before they coo again. Many birds swell out the throat when calling or singing, and others move it up and down ; these actions must also be caused by the swallowed air in the oesophagus, and must modify the sounds in some way as variously used, adding power and richness in some cases, or giving ventriloquistic effect in others. This question seems never to have been inquired into before ; and I throw out the hint in hopes that others may help to elucidate the matter with their investigations. As regards Pigeons, Mr. W. B. Tegetmeier suggests " that the dilated oesophagus and crop serve as a chamber of resonance, the air in             ##--SPLIT--##    which, being thrown into vibration, may be expelled to produce a louder sound."     83. Z, S. 1860, p. 349.     Phyllopneuste kennicotti, Baird, Trans. Chicago Acad. Sci. 1869, i. p. 313, Pl. 30. fig. 2.     This Willow- Wren passes through Amoy each spring and autumn in immense numbers j and I have a large series which vary in size and length of parts, but can barely be separated into large and small races, as intermediate sizes occur. I have seen our species from the Tenasserim province, and have two skins of the same from Flores, collected by Mr. Wallace, and marked S. flavescens, G. R. Gray. Dr. V. Schrenck has sent me the same from the Kurile Islands labelled Sylvia eversmanni ; and we have received many from Trans-Baikal through Madame Verdey of Paris. Further, I have examined two specimens from North Russia, collected by M. Mèves, and kindly lent rne by Messrs. Salvin arid Godman, to whom they belong. These are marked P. eversmanni, and are also identical with our China bird. Dr. Hartlaub's P. borealis is founded on a specimen from the shores of the sea of Okhotsk ; and on carefully reading his description of it, and comparing it with the so-called P. javanica (a Java specimen), J find no material difference between them, further than 1 can match from my series of specimens from China. Finally, Mr. Tristram insists that the type of P. kennicotti, Baird, from Alaska, which has been submitted to his inspection, is no other than our Chinese friend. This species thus shows itself to be a great wanderer, resorting in winter to the Malayan peninsula and archipelago, and seeking a summer home in the high latitudes of Asia, extending even to Russia (and apparently to Heligoland) on the European side, and to Alaska on the American side. During its migrations it occurs in Formosa ; and I have seen it in the Leyden Museum from Japan.     139. REGULOIDES PROREGULUS (Pall.), Ibis, 1867, p. 408, 1870, p. 345 ; P. S. 1870, p. 130.     The representative in Eastern Asia of M. alba, L., of Europe ; with clearer plumage, smaller bill, wing of a lighter brown, with much more white, especially on the wing-coverts. In full summer plumage the pectoral black contracts on the throat, and falls short of the chin. It has a very close ally in M. dukhunensis, Sykes, of  India, but may be distinguished from that by its larger bill, greater amount of white on the wing-coverts; and in nuptial dress by the form of the pectoral patch. Occasional specimens from Trans-Baikal have a black line uniting the back of the eye with the black of the nape, but otherwise similar to the typical bird. This I would propose to distinguish as var. temporalis. It will probably be the bird procured in Lombardy in the collection of Count Turati of Milan (see Baron de Selys-Longchamps in Ibis, 1870, p. 451). Numerous skins of the Baikal Wagtail have been received through  Madame Verdey of Paris ; and it is doubtless the species observed by me on the Upper Yangtsze (P. Z. S. 1870, p. 130).                         ##--SPLIT--##        196. MOTACILLA OCULARIS, Swinh. Ibis, 1863, pp. 94, 309; P. Z. It is a mistake to identify the European A. cecilii, Audouin (= A. rufogularis, Brehm), with our eastern A. cervinus. Ours is a smaller bird, with shorter wing, and in summer is easily distinguished by the rosy hue of its eyebrow and breast, which in the other are rust-colour, the breast being streaked with black. The streaks on the flanks, too, in the latter are much longer and broader.                         ##--SPLIT--##        209. Nat. 1788, p. 299 ; Ibis, 1860, p. 59, 1861, pp. 43, 255, 340, 1863, p. 272, 1866, pp. 135, 295, 394 ; P. Z. S. 1863, p. 286, 1870, p. 428.     Lanius schwaneri, Bp. Consp. Av. i. p. 363.     Upper parts light liver-brown ; forehead greyish white, with well-defined white eyebrow. Adult female resembles the male. The commonest species that passes through Amoy. Those collected on passage through Formosa are all immature, as if they had not strength to make the through voyage to the Philippines without rest. L. schwaneri of Borneo seems, from the description, to be based on a partially mature female of this species. Found in summer as far north as Talien Bay.         303. LANIUS INCERTUS, n. sp.     Crown, from beak to occiput, cinereous, with no eyebrow ; rest of upper parts reddish brown, brighter on the rump. One male specimen procured at Amoy from a party of the last. These four red-tailed Shrikes may be considered geographical races, or representative species, each reserving to itself an area in South-eastern Asia for its winter haunt, and another in North-eastern Asia for its summer haunt. The L. cristatus, that spreads throughout India in winter, seems to seek a family home in Dauria and the country directly north of India, extending to Trans- Baikal ; L. lucionensis travels across the sea from its warm winter retreat in the Philippines, and spends its summer in North China, extending its range to Talien Bay ; the southernmost species, L. superciliosus, comes from Malacca, and, passing the summer range of the latter, seeks the high latitudes of Amoorland and northern Japan as more suitable for the nurture of its offspring. As in the case of the Limicolce, those that go furthest north to breed, hurry furthest south to escape the rigours of winter; such is, apparently, the case with L. superciliosus. But this is scarcely a rule with land-birds; for this Shrike's winter compatriot, L. magnirostris, as I have shown (P. Z. S. 1870, p. 131), is contented to accommodate himself at a halfway station on the journey in Central China. The route taken by L. superciliosus is not down the China coast, or we should meet him straggling down in spring and autumn, as all the migrants do that travel by land. He probably takes the more inland course followed by many birds that summer in North China and the Amoor. How, then, does it occasionally turn up with the large flights of L. lucionensis bound to the Philippines and Borneo? How, further, does it happen that L. cristatus should step out of its way and straggle on a tour to a country widely separate from its regular winter quarters? I would suggest as an explanation that the routes travelled by the three species nust somewhere cross or touch, arid that the outliers of the bird-caravans would get attracted to the            ##--SPLIT--##     allied throng with which they came into contact, and so pass on with it inadvertently. Thus it is matter of no great surprise that the typical L. lucionensis is occasionally obtained, as Mr. Blyth informs me, in Ceylon, whither it would have travelled with L. cristatus, or in the Tenasserim, whither the company of L. superciliosus would have conducted it. To account for this phenomenon I was before led to suppose that the variation of the three species was not constantly fixed, and that each race occasionally developed the peculiarities of either of the others. But my present hypothesis is, I think, the right one. There need be no confusion of races by the intercrossing of species, for the misguided birds would be among strangers only for the winter ; in the summer, on their return journey, they would have the opportunity of rejoining their species.     Lanius incertus is founded on one male specimen, and, from its being so different in plumage, will, I think, be found to belong to another geographical race, with winter and summer resorts of its own, which has, in a similar way, been allured from its ordinary course of migration.         304. Ibis, 1860, p. 59, 1861, pp. 43, 340, 1863, p. 266, 1865, p. 348, 1866, p. 121. 1870, p. 244 ; P. Z. S. 1862, p. 319, 1863, p. 285, 1870, p. 433.     The term macrocercus has been applied in India to two distinct species of Black Drongos, the one a long bird,always distinguishable by a white spot close to the gape, the D. albirictus of Hodgson ; and the other allied to our Chinese bird. The name was given by Vieillot to the Java bird of this group the Edolius lonyus, Temm., and E. forficatus, Horsf. The Java species is smaller than the Indian bird, of more slender and elongated form, and has smaller feet. Our China bird, which is found throughout China, Hainan, and Formosa, is larger than its Indian ally, with longer bill and much longer wing, and has a rich bronze gloss over its feathers, including its wings and tail ; wing 6 inches, tail 6. I propose to separate it specifically under the above name. Our Drongo, in its nestling plumage, is of a greyish black (which browns with wear), deeper on the upper parts, bronzed on its wings and tail, with white on its axillaries and carpal edge. In changing into the bronzed plumage of the adult, the feathers of the underparts appear with broad white margins, which gradually give place to uniform bronze. Young males often begin to acquire the adult plumage on leaving the nest.                 ##--SPLIT--##    307. BUCHANGA MOUHOTI, Walden, Ann. Mag. N. S. 1863, p. 266.     South China, Hainan, and Formosa. Birds from the same locality extremely variable in size. Our birds agree with specimens from Bengal, Malacca, Java, and some of the isles. From Timor I have seen a larger species, the C. moluccensis, Bernst. (medius, G. R. Gr.), which by its similar immature plumage is connected with the present species, but in size almost approaches the foregoing. The second or streaked plumage of the C. bengalensis is peculiar for the enormous length of the upper tail-coverts, which nearly cover the surface of the tail to its end. In the first or nestling plumage, and in the adult or rufous-and-black plumage, these coverts are short. In this intermediate dress it is recognized as the C. lepidus of Horsfield.                                             ##--SPLIT--##        449. I included this species in my former catalogue (P. Z. S. 1863, p. 265) in the belief that I had an immature specimen of it from Shanghai ; but this last I now find belongs to the following species. It is not, improbable, however, that this species may occur in China.         457. CUCULUS MICRO PTERUS, Gould, P. Z. S. 1837, p. 137.     Cuculus affinis, A. Hay, J. A. S. B. xv. 18 (large variety).     Cuculus michieanus, Swinhoe, Ann. & Mag. Nat. Hist. vol. vi. 4th series, p. 153.     This very distinct species may at once be recognized by its brown back, wings, and tail, the latter with a broad black bar at the end, connecting the subgenus Hierococcyx with the true Cuckoos. It is almost divisible into two varieties, of larger and smaller form ; but these do not differ in notes according to Jerdon, nor indeed are they constant in size. I found the species on the Upper Yangtsze in company with the C. sparveroides, but not so common. Our birds are of the large form. They were very noisy, flying from tree to tree in an agitated manner, and uttering notes which are well syllabled in its Bengalese name, Bou-kotdko. I have an immature bird of the same from Shanghai, as noted under the foregoing species (P. Z. S. 1863, p. 265).         458. CUCULUS CANORUS, L. ; Ibis, 1860, p. 62, 1863, p. 96; P. Z. Ibis, 1863, p. 394.     I have a large series of this form of Cuckoo from Amoy, Formosa, and Pekin, and find them so variable in size, and in length and form of bill, that I give up in despair making species of them. From South Formosa I got a specimen with small bill and peculiar note ; this I described as C. monosyllabicus ; but it agrees with a specimen from Java labelled C. tenuirostris, Temm., sent me by Prof. Schlegel ; and a series from Amoy and a bird from Pekin are also the same. From North Formosa I procured birds with large and long bills. These I described as C. kelungensis, and I have a specimen from Amoy with the same features (noted as C. micropterus, P. Z. S. 1863, p. 265) ; but they do not possess a single other character to distinguish them. From Amoy I have one with a particularly short and broad bill, but also with no other character ; and a second with a thick curved bill, of large size, and with all the claws deep black. This last I determined as C. canoroides of S. Müller ; but on glancing  through my series I find many of the lesser and small-billed form, as also many of the long-billed form, with more or less black claws, in fact almost every gradation from pure yellow to black. I come therefore (I must confess, most reluctantly) to the conclusion that they all belong to the same variable species.     Like all the Cuckoos, these birds in China are only summer visitants.         461. Smith, Zool. of South Africa; Jerdon, B. of Ind. iii. p. 600.     In the winter 1868-69, when I was last in Formosa, I procured several females and one male of the little Turnix that abounds on the low grassy hills about fifteen miles from Takow. It had escaped me before, but its eggs I had got in abundance. This I cannot distinguish from the small Button-Quail of India. The females were bigger than the male, but of siinilar plumage. They both had deep-bluish bills, and the former bluish grey legs, the legs of the male being more flesh-coloured. This bird has not yet been met with in China.         511. Frequents the lowest hill-ranges of South Formosa, showing a preference for rocky places covered with scrub. The female is much larger than the male, and acquires a black throat in summer.         512. AREOTURNIX BLAKISTONI, n. sp.     Turnix ocellata, Ibis, 1866, p. 131 ; P. Z. S. 1863, p. 308.     A male specimen of the T. ocellata group was procured by Capt. Blakiston at Canton, and kindly given to me. This differs too much from the last for me to consider it of the same species. Its nearest ally is the T. pugnax, Temm., of Malacca, but it is smaller, shorter-toed, and possesses a remarkably small bill. Instead of spots                                     ##--SPLIT--##    it has numerous bands across the breast ; and its upper parts are very rufous. I propose to separate it under the name of its discoverer.         513. HEMIPODIUS VICIARIUS, n. sp.     Turnix jondera, Hodgs. ? ; Swinh. Ibis, 1861, p. 50.     Turnix dussumieri, Ibis, 1861, p. 341.     Turnix maculosa, Ibis, 1866, p. 131 ; P. Z. S.  1863, p. 308, 1870, p. 442.     The Indian species of this group is the Turnix jondera, Hodgs., figured in Gray and Mitchell's * Genera of Birds' (T. dussumieri, Jerd. B. of India, iii. p. 599) ; and a similar but larger bird is found in Burmah (T. blanfordi, Blyth, J. A. S. B. 1863, p. 80). The latter from its size (wing 4 inches) seems to answer to the original Hemipodius maculosus,Temm. Pig. et Gall. iii. p. 631, 1815 (maculatus, Vieill. Gal. des Ois. 1834, iv. p. 51, Pl. 217), the locality of which is doubtfully given as New Holland. Our bird, which ranges from Canton to Pekin, differs from these in wanting the red collar and the median stripe on the crown. The feathers on the crown are black margined with reddish brown, the breast is bright buff; the feathers of the back and rump are blotched and finely waved with black, and most of them have long ochreous spots on their margins. It is about the size of T. blanfordi, and in general plumage pretty similar. Irides white. Upper mandible brown ; lower mandible and legs ochreous yellow. The females are rather larger than the males, but do not seem to differ in plumage. The gizzard of a female was large and heart-shaped ; caeca long and bulging ; its trachea swollen above the junction with the bronchi.         514. OTIS TARDA, L. ; P. Z. I have one shot at Canton by Mr. S. Bligh. It is of large form, with long and large bill, white on the frontal                             ##--SPLIT--##    feathers at the base of the bill, of a pale colour with blotches of black on the underparts, and with very little grey on the wing- coverts and rump. The size of the bill varies greatly in English specimens ; but the pale colour, comparative absence of grey, and white frontal edging to the bill seem characteristic of the Eastern race, and I think it as well to distinguish it as a variety.         627. S.  1863, p. 323.     Anser serrirostris, Gould, MS.     A Bean-Goose comes down in immense swarms every cold weather to the marshes, at the river's mouth, near Amoy. They are of large size, and peculiar in having huge bills approximating that of A. grandis. I have handled several, and they were all so distinguishable. I have brought home a male and compared it with a home-shot bird kindly lent me by Mr. Tristram. The Chinese bird has a paler cheek and throat, a much lighter and yellowish-brown neck, narrower white edges and tips to the tail-feathers, and longer tarsi ; but in other respects it is similar. The British Museum has two specimens of this Goose from Trans-Baikal, and Mr. Gould a specimen from Shanghai. Mr. Gould's specimen is labelled A. serrirostris, a name he intended to publish in 1862. On the Wanchow river all the Geese shot by our party on the 10th of February were of this race.         628. ANAS BOSCHAS, L. ; Ibis, 1861, p. 344, 1862, p. 254.     A winter Duck in South China.         629. AN.AS ZONORHYNCHA, Swinh. Ibis, 1866, p. 394 ; P. Z. S. 1870, p. 427.    Anas pcecilorhyncha, Temm. & Schl. Faun. Jap. pl. lxxxii.     Throughout China. I believe it breeds in many parts, north and south. It is absurd, with our present experience, to imagine this bird a cross between A. boschas and A. pcecilorhyncha of India. It is a very common Duck at Shanghai, and I have seen many specimens. A. poecilorhyncha does not occur with us; and can we suppose that India would originate a bird to disperse throughout more Eastern Asia, and not reserve any of the hybrids for herself ? Our bird (male) has a narrower yellow nib to the bill, and no basal yellow. It has a distinct white supercilium ; throat unspeckled white ; alar speculum deep blue, slightly tinged with green ; lesser coverts, overlapping the speculum, brown, tipped with black with no white ; tertials only slightly margined with white ; rump, upper tail-coverts, and tail deep blackish brown, without any bronze reflections ; crown, streak through the eye, and short moustache-streak deep brown ; cheeks and neck brownish white, with brown specks ; breast light yellowish brown, with deep-brown spots ; rest of underparts deep brown, becoming nearly black on the under tail-coverts ;                                             ##--SPLIT--##    axillaries pure white. Size about that of A. poecilorhyncha. The female is smaller than the male, and has the blotched yellowish of the breast extending to the abdomen. In A. poecilorhyncha the breast and belly are nearly white, covered with round brown spots. The speculum of our bird is greener in some specimens ; and some show a little white on the overlapping wing-coverts in rear of their black tips, and have more or less white on the outer webs of the tertials.         630. It was being hawked about in the streets. My specimen answers precisely to Radde's description and figure, and looks very much like a cross between Anas boschas and Fulix cristata, having somewhat the head and breast of the former and the body of the latter. But mine is too exact a copy of Radde's type, which he procured from a party of four of the same, to admit of being considered a hybrid. Bill French grey. Irides light yellow. ##--SPLIT--##出自《倫敦動物學會集刊》，1871年5月2日出版。中國與其島嶼之鳥類目錄修正版，包括新種描述、過往注釋參照與評註。作者	倫敦動物學學會會員羅伯特・史溫侯。1. 胡兀鷲（Gypaetus barbatus, Cuv.）；1867年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第413頁；1870年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第430頁    我曾在隸屬北京天主教傳教會的博物館裏看到過胡兀鷲的標本，是由阿曼德・大衛神父在北京西邊的山上取得的。大英博物館也有一件胡兀鷲的標本，上面標著「中國」。##--SPLIT--##    2. 禿鷲（Vultur monachus, L.，目前學名Aegypius monachus）；1867年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第413頁    北京博物館有這種兀鷲的標本，是大衛神父在直隸省1西北的山上取得的。3. 金鵰（Aquila chrysaetos, Cuv.）；1870年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第443頁    在從北京前往蒙古的途中，我曾在居庸關看到過幾次鷹，不過無法確定是哪個物種。根據大衛神父的鑑識，這些山區的留鳥為金鵰，並提到曾看到過各體脂機的變異相當大，有較長鳥喙與白色跗蹠（〈北京鳥類誌〉，1867年《巴黎自然史博物館新檔案》卷3），而格尼也告訴我，牠曾經在丹麥與阿爾及利亞都看過這種鳥。格尼補充說，「這些標本在其他方面都是第二齡的常態羽衣。丹麥標本又大又好，阿爾及利亞的標本則不是特別好。目前我還沒有看到足夠的變異，讓我認為金鵰可分為2種。」4. 白肩鵰（Aquila bifasciata, J. E. Gray.，目前學名Aquila heliaca）異名：Aquila heliaca，1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第259頁；1865年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第347頁    霍華德・桑德斯（Howard Saunders）曾向學會2證明，東方型的白肩鵰至少在第一年羽衣上與歐洲型不同，而且顯然是不同的物種。在講到大衛神父從北京送去的一件鳥類標本時，米爾恩-愛德華茲（M. Milne-Edwards）提到葛雷博士（Gray）的名字，這位神父提到這隻鳥時表示，「在我們與蒙古的山區很常見。」有人曾在冬天時，於福州射殺了一隻帶有未成鳥羽衣的個體；我在福爾摩沙西南部打狗的猴山（今柴山）上曾觀察到，成鳥羽毛的肩部為白色。5. 阿穆爾鵰（Aquila amurensis，新種）類似花雕（Aquila clanga, Pall.，目前學名Clanga clanga）不過體型較大。    米爾恩-愛德華茲將來自北京的草原鵰鑑定為帕拉斯（Pallas）的花雕；大衛神父還注意到，同一地區也有體型較小的近緣種印度烏鵰（Aquila naevia, Briss，目前學名Clanga hastata）。施倫克博士（Dr. v. Schrenck）好像從黑龍江給我送了一件標本，牠的體型很大，而且就格尼看來，可以分成這個類群的第三種。我將這種鳥列入目前的名錄中，這是假定牠與大衛神父目錄中的第5號相同，而大衛神父也表示，這種鳥「在我們的山區與蒙古很常見。」格尼將阿穆爾的標本與來自窩瓦河薩雷普塔3的一隻花雕雌鳥進行比較，並給了我以下的測量結果：體長翼長（自腕關節）尾長跗蹠中趾長（不含爪）花鵰31英吋23英吋11英吋31/2英吋21/4英吋阿穆爾鵰331/2英吋261/4英吋121/2英吋41/2英吋21/4英吋    格尼寫道：「在羽衣方面，除了有顏色很淺的橫紋、次級飛羽的橫紋顏色較淺、因此，除了外側羽片更加明顯之外，我並沒有發現牠和花雕有何不同。」##--SPLIT--##        「看起來，這裏的鵰有3種，牠們只有在相對體形上有些不同：」    「印度烏鵰是3種中最小的一種，發現於埃及、南歐、士麥那、巴勒斯坦、印度與蘇門答臘（一件標本）。」    「花鵰的體形介於中間，出現在窩瓦河口的薩雷普塔。牠和印度烏鵰的標本都曾在土耳其的歐洲部分與巴勒斯坦取得。我也曾在美索不達米亞看過薩雷普塔種，不過牠的分布範圍究竟往東延伸到哪裡，我並不清楚。」    「阿穆爾鵰。體型最大者。我不知道其分布範圍往西延伸到何處。」6. 白尾海鵰（Haliaeetus albicilla. L.）；1865年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第347頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第259頁    分布於揚子江以北，偶爾才會出現在揚子江以南。在1867-1868年冬天，一隻亞成鳥飛到汕頭港的一艘船上，被抓了起來，輾轉來到我手上。牠的虹膜是清澈的黃褐色，蠟膜為淺藍灰色，腳為檸檬黃色，爪為黑色。7. 虎頭海鵰（Haliaeetus pelagicus, 在北京西邊的山區繁殖，於9月大量聚集。霍奇森繪製的尼泊爾個體（陳列於大英博物館）顯示，會飛去印度的正是這種黃爪隼。    成年雄鳥的所有覆羽一直到肩羽都是灰色的；內側或短初級飛羽尖端羽緣有大片泛白，在亞成鳥身上則為紅褐色；羽軸下側白色部分比尖端短了31/4英吋。22. 東方蜂鷹（Pernis apivorus, L.，目前學名Pernis ptilorhynchus）；大衛《北京鳥類誌》 B. Tegetmeier）建議道，「擴張的食道與嗉囊就像共鳴腔，##--SPLIT--##    裏面震動的空氣被排出去，發出更大的聲音。」83. 戴勝印度亞種（Upupa ceylonensis, Reichenbach，目前學名Upupa epops ceylonensis）；布萊斯（Blyth），1866年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第366頁，1870年刊第91頁異名：Upupa longirostris, Jerdon，《印度鳥類誌》（Birds of India）卷1第393頁。    可見於海南。也出現在爪哇與錫蘭（卡巴尼斯），以及暹羅和緬甸。我的海南標本與布萊斯寄給我的緬甸標本具有相同的特徵。84. 叉尾太陽鳥（Aethopyga christinae, Swinh.），《自然史年鑑暨雜誌》第4輯卷4第436頁；1870年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第236頁。    可見於海南。85. 藍喉太陽鳥（Aethopyga abrii, J. 可能會誤認為那種鳥。米爾恩-愛德華茲因此將之描述為：「上半身為橄欖褐色調；灰白色眼線從喙基部延伸到後頸，然後向另一側類似的條紋彎曲。喉部灰白色；胸部與腹部的側面帶明顯褐色調，往中間則為大黃色調的灰白色。跗蹠帶黃色。」在北京附近的高山築巢（大衛）。133. 褐色柳鶯（Phyllopneuste fuscata, Blyth，目前學名Phylloscopus Av.）卷1第290頁。Sylvia （Phyllopneuste） eversmanni, Midd.（非Bonap.），《西伯利亞遊記》，沃格特，第178頁，表16圖1-3>Sylvia flavescem, G. R. Gray，1860年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第349頁。Phyllopneuste kennicotti, Baird，1869年《芝加哥科學院學報》（Trans. Chicago Acad. Sci.）卷1第313頁插圖30圖2。    這種柳鶯會在春季與秋季大批經過廈門；我搜集了許多標本，牠們的大小與身體部位的長度各不相同，但幾乎無法區分成體型大與體型小的種，因為中間尺寸確實存在。我曾看過來自丹那沙林的極北柳鶯，也有2件華萊士在弗洛勒斯島採集並標示為「S. flavescens, G. R. Gray」的標本。施倫克博士從千島群島寄來了同種鳥的標本，標示的是「Sylvia eversmanni」；我們也從巴黎韋迪女士（Madame Verdey）處獲得了許多來自外貝加爾山脈的標本。此外，我還檢查了2件梅維斯（Mèves）在俄羅斯北部收集到的標本，牠們的所有人是賽爾溫（Salvin）與哥德曼（Godman）兩位先生。這些標本上標示的是「P. eversmanni」，與我們在中國看到的鳥一樣。哈特勞布博士（Hartlaub）的「P. borealis」是基於一件來自鄂霍次克海沿岸的標本；仔細閱讀他的描述並與所謂的「P. javanica」（一件來自爪哇的標本）進行比較後，我並沒在兩者之間發現實質的差別，而我從我的中國系列標本中能找到的差別還比較大。最後，崔斯坦堅持，經他之手檢查的阿拉斯加「P. kennicotti, Baird」標本也與我們的中國朋友同種。因此，這種鳥是範圍極廣的遊蕩型候鳥，冬季會飛往馬來半島與馬來群島，夏季則會去亞洲高緯度地區避暑，甚至會去到俄羅斯的歐洲部分（顯然延伸到黑爾戈蘭），以及美國的阿拉斯加。在遷徙過程中，牠會出現在福爾摩沙；我也曾在萊登博物館（Leyden Museum）看到過來自日本的標本。139. 黃腰柳鶯（Reguloides proregulus, lucionensis），格梅林，1788年《自然系統》第299頁；1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第59頁，1861年刊第43、255、340頁，1863年第272頁，1866年第135、295、394頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第286頁，1870年刊第428頁。異名：Lanius schwaneri, Bp.，《鳥的形象》卷1第363頁。    上半身為淺栗褐色；前額灰白色，有明顯的白色眉線。成年雌鳥外觀與雄鳥類似。這是廈門最常見的候鳥。在飛經福爾摩沙時採集到的標本，全都是未成鳥，似乎不在福爾摩沙休息一下，就沒力氣完成飛抵菲律賓的行程。從描述來看，婆羅洲的「L. schwaneri」似乎是這個物種接近完全成熟的雌鳥。夏季最北可在大連灣發現其蹤跡。303. 伯勞（Lanius incertus，新種）21    從喙到枕部的頭冠部分為灰色，沒有眉線；上半身其餘部分為紅褐色，尾部顏色較明亮。有只在廈門取得的雄鳥標本，原本與一群紅尾伯勞一起活動。這4種尾羽紅色的伯勞可以被認為是地理區域的代表物種，牠們分別到東南亞的不同地方度冬，在東北亞的不同地方度夏。紅尾伯勞在冬天遍及整個印度地區，似乎在達烏里山脈與緊鄰印度北界的地區，一直延伸到外貝加爾山脈的整片地區；紅尾伯勞呂宋亞種會從溫暖的菲律賓度冬地越洋而來，在華北一直延伸到大連灣的這個區域渡過夏天；遷徙範圍最南的紅尾伯勞日本亞種，會從麻六甲出發，越過紅尾伯勞呂宋亞種的夏季分布範圍，前往黑龍江流域與日本北部等高緯度地區來繁殖育雛。就如涉禽的狀況，那些到最北地區去繁殖的物種，為躲避嚴冬，會匆忙到最南的地方去度冬；紅尾伯勞日本亞種顯然就是這個狀況。然而，這樣的規則並不適用於陸鳥；如我所示（1870年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第131頁），這種伯勞的冬季夥伴虎紋伯勞「L. magnirostris」22在華中地區遷徙時，會在中途停留。紅尾伯勞日本亞種的路線並不是沿著中國海岸，否則我們應該會在春秋兩季零星觀察到個體，就如所有經由陸路遷徙的候鳥一樣。牠的遷徙路線可能更靠內陸，那是許多在華北地區與黑龍江流域度夏的許多鳥類所遵循的路線。那麼，牠為何偶爾會與飛往菲律賓與婆羅洲的紅尾伯勞呂宋亞種一起出現呢？此外，紅尾伯勞在遷徙過程中是否會偏離原本的路線，遊蕩到與通常的度冬地完全不同的地方去？我的解釋是，這3種鳥的遷徙路線必然會在某的地方交叉或是非常接近，群鳥中總有一些會受到接觸的近緣種所吸引，##--SPLIT--##    在不經意的狀況下轉移到另一群。因此，正如布萊斯告訴我的那樣，在錫蘭偶爾會發現可能與紅尾伯勞一起飛抵的紅尾伯勞呂宋亞種，或是在丹那沙林偶爾可以看到可能隨著日本亞種一起飛抵的呂宋亞種個體，都不足為奇。為了解釋這個現象，我曾假定這3種鳥的變化並不是固定的，每種鳥偶爾都會發展出另一種的特徵。然而，我現在認為我的假說才是正確的。各種之間並沒有種間配對造成的混淆，因為迷鳥只有在冬天才會和陌生的夥伴待在一起；到了夏天，在返回的途中，牠們會有機會重新加入各自所屬的鳥種。    這種伯勞這個鳥種是以一件雄鳥標本為根據而建立的，由於牠的羽衣如此不同，我認為牠應該屬於另一個地理族群，有獨自的度冬地與度夏地，並以類似的方式在遷徙過程中被吸引過來。304. 鉤嘴林鵙喜馬拉雅亞種（Tephrodornis pelvica, Hodgs.，目前學名Tephrodornis virgatus Müll.       Cuculus optatus, Gould.       Cuculus canorus，1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第396頁。       Cuculus monosyllabicus, Swinh.，1865年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第545頁。       Cuculus kelungensis, Gould，1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第394頁。    我從廈門、福爾摩沙與北京採集到大量中杜鵑標本，發現牠們在體型大小、喙長與喙形的變化非常大，讓我絕望地放棄了為牠們分類的工作。我從福爾摩沙南部得到的標本喙小，而且叫聲獨特，我將牠稱為「C. monosyllabicus」；然而，牠在外觀上與施萊格爾教授從爪哇寄給我的「C. tenuirostris, Temm.」標本類似，而另一批來自廈門的標本，以及另一只來自北京的標本，也具有相同的特徵。我從福爾摩沙北部取得的標本有更大更長的喙，我將牠們描述為「C. kelungensis」，不過我也有另一只廈門標本具有同樣的特徵（被描述為「C. micropterus」〔1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第265頁〕）；然而牠們並沒有任何其他足以將牠們區別出來的特徵。我有一只廈門標本的喙特別短也特別寬，不過也沒有可供區別的其他特徵；另一只廈門標本的喙又厚又彎，挺大，而且所有爪都是黑色，我將牠認定為穆勒（S. Müller）的「C. canoroides」，不過當我瀏覽我的系列收藏時，也發現許多體型較小喙較小的個體，也有許多長喙的個體，或多或少都有黑色爪子，而且事實上，爪色從純黃色到黑色中間的每個層次都有。我因此勉強得到的結論是，這些個體都是同一種鳥的變種。    就如所有杜鵑，這些鳥在中國只是夏候鳥。461. 紅頭綠鳩（Treron formosae, Swinh.），1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第396頁，1865年刊第540頁，1866年刊第312頁。    可見於福爾摩沙南部。462. 綠鳩（Sphenocercus sororius, Swinh.，目前學名Treron sieboldii），1866年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第311、406頁。異名：Sphenocercus formosae，雄鳥，1866年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第122頁。	Treron chaeroboatis，1866年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第313、406頁    可見於福爾摩沙南部。就如日本的綠鳩，不過頭部顏色更黃，背部、翅膀與尾巴的綠色更乾淨，沒有帶著褐油色色調。463. 橙胸綠鳩海南亞種（Osmotreron domvilii, Swinh.，目前學名Treron bicinctus domvilii），1870年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第354頁。    可見於海南。464. 綠皇鳩雲南亞種（Carpophaga sylvatica, Tick.，目前學名Ducula aenea sylvatica）；1870年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第355頁。異名：Columba sylvatica, Tickell，《亞洲學會期刊》卷2第581頁。    可見於海南。465. 野鴿（Columba livia, Briss.），《Av.》卷1第82頁；1870年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第444頁。    在北京平原一帶的野生鳥類。##--SPLIT--##466. 岩鴿（Columba rupestris, Bp.），《鳥的形象》卷2第48頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第306頁，1870年刊第434頁。異名：Columba aenas, var. rupestris, Pall.，《俄羅斯-亞洲動物志》表35Columba leucozonura, Swinh.，1861年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第259頁，1863年刊第88頁。    可見於華北地區。467. 斑林鴿（Dendrotreron hodgsonii, Vig.，目前學名Columba hodgsonii）；波拿巴第《鴿子群像》（Ic. 1868-69年冬天，在我最後一次前往福爾摩沙時，我獲得了幾隻小型三趾鶉的標本，包括幾隻雌鳥與一隻雄鳥，這種鳥在距離打狗約15英里處的低矮丘陵草地一帶數量很多。我曾經差一點抓到這種鳥，卻讓牠逃過一劫，不過我倒是採集了許多鳥蛋。我無法將牠和印度的小型三趾鶉區分開來。林三趾鶉的雌鳥體型比雄鳥大，不過羽色相近，都有深藍色的喙，雌鳥的腿為藍灰色，雄鳥則更偏肉色。這種鳥還沒有在中國發現。511. ocellatuss）為近緣種，不過體型大得多，喙與腿也更有力。經常於福爾摩沙南部的低矮丘陵地活動，偏好有灌木覆蓋的岩石地形。雌鳥的體型比雄鳥大得多，而且喉嚨在夏季會變成黑色。512. 棕三趾鶉支那亞種（Areoturnix blakistoni，新種，目前學名Turnix suscitator blakistoni）異名：Turnix 下半身羽色淺帶黑斑，覆羽與尾部有少量灰色。在英國標本中，喙的大小差異很大；不過蒼白的體色、相對欠缺灰色、以及喙的白色前緣似乎是東方種的特徵，我認為牠應該視為一個種。627. 凍原豆雁（Anser segetum, var. serrirostris，目前學名Anser serrirostris）異名：Anser fabalis）的大型喙。我經手過的標本有好幾件，牠們都有非常明顯的特徵。我帶了1件雄鳥標本回家，將牠與崔斯坦好心出借給我、在家鄉射殺的標本進行比較。中國個體的臉頰與喉嚨顏色較淺，頸部黃褐色較淺，尾羽的白色羽緣與尖端都比較窄，跗蹠較長；不過就其他方面來說則很類似。大英博物館有2件來自外貝加爾山脈的凍原豆雁標本，顧爾德有1件來自上海的標本。顧爾德的標本被歸類為「A. serrirostris」，他打算在1862年發表這個學名。2月10日在溫州河被我們隊上射殺的雁，全都是凍原豆雁。628. 綠頭鴨（Anas boschas, L.，目前學名Anas platyrhynchos），1861年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第344頁，1862年刊第254頁。    華南地區的冬候鳥。629. 花嘴鴨（Anas zonorhyncha, poecilorhyncha）的雜交種是荒謬的。這種鴨在上海很常見，我曾看過許多標本。印度花嘴鴨並沒有出現在中國；更何況，我們能假設印度會出現一種能散佈到東亞地區，而在印度本土卻完全看不到的雜交種嗎？我們的鳥（雄鳥）有更窄的黃色喙尖，喙的基部沒有黃色。牠有明顯的白色眼上剛毛；喉部為無斑點的白色；翼斑深藍色，稍帶綠色調；小覆羽蓋在翼斑上，呈褐色，尖端黑色無白色；三級飛羽略帶白色羽緣；臀部、尾上覆羽與尾巴為深黑褐色，沒有青銅色澤；有眼線，深褐色鬍狀短條紋；頰與頸為帶褐色的白色，帶褐色斑點；胸部為淺黃褐色帶深褐色斑點；下半身其餘部分為深褐色，到尾下覆羽幾乎變成黑色；脇部羽毛為純白色。##--SPLIT--##    體型與印度花嘴鴨差不多。雌鳥比雄鳥小，胸部有黃色斑點延伸到下腹部，而印度花嘴鴨的胸部與腹部幾乎是白色，並帶有褐色圓點。花嘴鴨有些個體的翼斑顏色更綠，有些在覆羽交疊處、黑色尖端後面帶有少許白色，而且三級飛羽的外側羽瓣多少帶點白色。630. 花鳧（Tadorna cornuta, Gmel.，目前學名Tadorna tadorna）異名：Tadorna vulpanser, Pall.，目前學名Hydroprogne caspia），1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第328頁。異名：Sterna caspia, Pall.；1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第68頁，1861年刊第345頁，1863年刊第430頁，1870年刊第366頁。	Sterna melanotis, Swainson（冬羽）。    一般而言可見於中國。663. 黑腹燕鷗（Hydrochelidon hybrida, L.，目前學名Chlidonias hybrida）異名：Hydrochelidon indica, Steph.；1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第428頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第328頁。	Sterna leucopareia, Natterer。    可見於福爾摩沙。尚未在中國發現。664. 黑浮鷗（Hydrochelidon nigra, L.，目前學名Chlidonias niger），1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第97頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第328頁。異名：Hydrochelidon javanica，1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第68頁，1861年刊第345頁。    分布遍及全中國。##--SPLIT--##665. 鳳頭燕鷗（Thalasseus pelecanoides, King，目前學名Thalasseus bergii）；施萊格爾，荷蘭博物館。異名：Thalasseus cristatus，1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。	Sterna cristata，1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第68頁，1863年刊第430頁。Sterna velox，1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第429頁，1861年刊第345頁，1866年刊第134頁。    可見於華南地區、福爾摩沙。在福爾摩沙北邊的島嶼上繁殖。666. 白眉燕鷗（Haliplana anaetheta, Scop.，目前學名Onychoprion anaethetus）異名：Sterna panayensis, Gmel.，1788年《自然系統》卷1第607頁。    常見於漁翁島（澎湖群島）。667. 燕鷗（Sterna hirundo, L.），1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。    可見於漢口、北京。668. 蒼燕鷗（Onychoprion melanauchen, Temm.，目前學名Sterna sumatrana），1867年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第230頁，1870年刊第367頁。異名：Sterna minuta?，1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第429頁。    常見於廈門港外的岩石上，牠們在那裏繁殖。669. 小燕鷗（Sterna minuta, L.，目前學名 Sternula albifrons），1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第430頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。異名：Sternula sinensis, Gmel.，1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第429頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。Sterna minuta，1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第68頁，1861年刊第345頁，1862年刊第307頁。    可見於福爾摩沙、中國。在福爾摩沙東岸繁殖。670. 白燕鷗（Gygis alba, Sparrm.）異名：Sterna candida, Gmel.，《自然系統》卷1第607頁；1864年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第423頁。    曾於中國南海西沙群島附近觀察到。671. 玄燕鷗指名亞種（Anous stolidus, L.，目前學名 Anous stolidus），1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第429頁，1863年刊第430頁，1864年刊第422頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。異名：Anous pileatus，《亞州學會華北分會期刊》1869年5月刊；1869年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第360頁。    可見於福爾摩沙東北部，也是其繁殖地。672. 黑叉尾海燕（Thalassidroma monorhis, Swinh.，目前學名Oceanodroma monorhis），1867年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第386頁。    可見於廈門。在福爾摩沙東北方的小島上繁殖（Collingwood 科林伍德）。673. 短尾信天翁（Diomedea albatrus, Pall.，目前學名Phoebastria albatrus），《俄羅斯-亞洲動物志》卷2第308頁；1864年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第423頁。異名：Diomedea brachyura, Temm.；1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第67頁，1863年刊第431頁，1867年刊第226頁，1870年刊第366頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。    可見於中國海域。674. 黑腳信天翁（Diomedea nigripes, Audubon，目前學名Phoebastria nigripes），1863年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第431頁；1863年《倫敦動物學會集刊》第329頁。異名：Diomedea fulginosa，1860年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第68頁。    可見於中國海域。##--SPLIT--##675. 軍艦鳥（Attagen minor, Gmel.，目前學名Fregata minor）異名：Pelecanus minor, Gmel.，1788年《自然系統》卷1第572頁。	Tachypetes minor，1868年《英國鳥類科學期刊》第56頁。    可見於西沙群島。曾在廈門射殺過一隻。備註：我曾在中國的《本草綱目》一書中發現所謂「飛獸」的圖像，明顯與歐文教授（Owen）描述的始祖鳥化石非常相像。我將在近期回到中國時進行調查。1 譯註：清朝內地的一個省，轄區大體為今河北省、北京市與天津市。也就是現在的河北省。2 原註：anteà第37頁3 譯註：今伏爾加格勒。4 譯註：今緬甸德林達依省。5 審定註：臺灣及海南之黑鳶，日後被歸類為Milvus migrans formosanus。6 審定註：這需進一步確認。7 譯註：目前為無效學名，實指金腰燕（學名Cecropis daurica）。8 譯註：此為無效學名，實為黑胸蜂虎。9 譯註：此為無效學名，實為黑胸蜂虎10 譯註：Zoographia Rosso-Asiatica11 譯註：歐洲鳥類學期刊。12 譯註：棕胸岩鷚的異名。13 譯註：日本岩鷚的異名。14 審定註：無效學名。15 審定註：無效學名。16 譯註：目前認為A. cecilii, Audouin為赤喉鷚的異名，赤喉鷚雖然分布廣泛，但沒有亞種分化。17 譯註：此為栗耳鳳鶥的同物異名。18 審訂註：繡眼畫眉目前已經為臺灣特有種，不屬於白眶雀鶥的亞種。19 譯註：今越南南部。20 原註：《自然史年鑑暨雜誌》卷17第2輯第78頁。ED21 審定註：應該是紅尾伯勞的某個亞種，但現今已成為無效名。22 譯註：此為虎紋伯勞的同物異名。23 譯註：指中亞的裏海至東北亞韃靼海峽一帶。24 審定註：目前認定為同物種。25 審定註：目前海南島的是小啄木海南亞種 Yungipicus canicapillus swinhoei。26 審定註：目前共有三種：北方中杜鵑、喜瑪拉雅中杜鵑和巽他中杜鵑，無法確認當時是指哪一種。27 譯註：Iconographie des pigeons.28 審定註：實為東北部。29 譯註：Bulletin de la Société d'acclimatation30 譯註：Histoire naturelle generale des pigeons et des gallinaces31 譯註：La galerie des oiseaux32 譯註：目前認為此為池鷺的異名。33 譯註：應是在孟加拉吉大港的東邊的一處地名。34 譯註：Abhandlungen der Königlichen Akademie der Wissenschaften zu Berlin------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

plans.name: 19世紀西方台灣書寫史料翻譯整理計畫
plans.description:
本計畫翻譯法文史料與英文史料。法文史料《法軍遠征福爾摩沙1884-1885》作者手稿及其附錄《法軍遠征福爾摩沙1884-1885 》地圖手繪稿，為法國軍官嘉諾（Eugène Germain Garnot）紀錄清法戰爭期間在雞籠、淡水、澎湖等地的見聞。此書為清法戰爭現存最詳盡的西文資料，1894年曾於法國出版，1960年由臺灣銀行經濟研究室出版中譯本《法軍侵台始末》，是研究清法戰爭臺灣之役的重要參考。此外，英文史料為臺灣自然史相關論文30篇，是英國博物學家史溫侯（Robert Swinhoe, 1836-1877，亦翻譯為郇和、斯文豪、士委諾）在19世紀中葉擔任英國駐臺領事期間所撰寫的，紀錄臺灣的自然生態與物產，是當時西方世界了解臺灣的重要資料。另外本案包含史溫侯所撰臺灣相關文章清單1份，是費德廉老師所整理的。

完成年份：2020年

計畫執行團隊
內容力有限公司
